title: Needle-felted great crested newt
subtitle: A dark, bumpy-skinned newt with orange belly markings in merino roving
excerpt: Build a great crested newt in dark grey-brown and vivid orange merino, with the characteristic bumpy skin texture and orange-and-black spotted belly. The finished newt is about 10 cm long and lies flat or poses on a small branch.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional needle-felting practice. Amphibian forms are a standard British needle-felting beginner project; the bumpy-skin texture technique follows standard guild workshop method.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method using barbed needles to tangle wool fibres together without heat or water. Each stab locks fibres into a progressively firmer mass.
Wool fibre drawn into a continuous ribbon. The standard input material for needle felting.
The progressive firming of needle-felted wool as more stitches are worked into the same area. Higher compaction produces a harder, denser surface.

content.text: The great crested newt is one of the most recognisable British amphibians, and its dark warty body translates well into
. The body is built in dark grey-brown
; the orange belly with black spots is added last. The bumpy skin texture comes from deliberately incomplete
and tiny added roving blobs on the back, then shallow surface needling to set the bumps without flattening them.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Keep a finger guard on the supporting hand at all times.

items.name: Black merino roving (tiny amount for spots)

content.text: Step 1: Build the body and tail

Roll grey-brown roving into a tapered sausage about 12 cm long: thicker at the shoulder end, narrowing to a flat tail. Needle felt firmly along the length until the body holds its shape without unrolling. Keep the surface slightly rough at this stage rather than smooth.

Roll four short stubby legs from grey-brown roving, each about 2 cm long. Needle felt each leg to a reasonable firmness, then attach them in pairs: front legs behind the head, hind legs at the mid-body. Needle through the leg base and into the body repeatedly until the join is secure. Legs should project outward at roughly 45 degrees.

Turn the newt over. Needle a patch of orange roving along the underside from chest to base of tail, leaving a narrow grey border at each side. Then needle tiny black spots randomly over the orange using very small wisps of black roving. The spots should be about 2 to 3 mm across.
Step 4: Add back texture and eyes

Place very small wisps of grey-brown roving on the back and needle each one lightly with just 3 to 4 stabs to set it as a bump rather than needling it flat. Work along the spine and flanks for a warty appearance. Finally, stitch black seed bead eyes in position just behind the nostrils.

items.problem: The bumps on the back flattened when I kept needling
items.cause: Over-needling the surface compacts the texture away
items.fix: Place the roving blobs for bumps and work each one with only 3 or 4 shallow stabs, then move to the next bump. Resist the urge to smooth the surface.

items.problem: The legs point in random directions instead of outward
items.cause: The legs were not positioned and held in the correct angle during attachment
items.fix: Hold each leg in the desired angle with one hand and stab through it into the body with the other. Stab from several directions to lock the angle before releasing.
